This vision tears away the pretense that our righteousness is our own achievement. Joshua stands in filthy garments before the angel of the LORD—not because he has been careless, but because that is the true condition of even the high priest, the one who mediates between God and His people. Satan's accusation is relentless and, by the standards of the law, legitimate. Yet the LORD does not defend Joshua on the grounds that he is actually clean. Instead, the LORD rebukes Satan and removes the filthy garments entirely, replacing them with splendid robes and a clean turban. This is not improvement. This is replacement. Joshua did not earn or deserve this restoration; the LORD accomplished it as an act of sovereign choice. The vision shows us that our standing before God is never sustained by our own performance, no matter how carefully we keep His laws or how high our office.

But notice what follows the cleansing. The angel does not leave Joshua adorned and silent. Instead, Joshua is given conditions: "If you walk in My ways and keep My instructions, then you will govern My house and will also have charge of My courts." Obedience is not the ground of acceptance—the filthy garments have already been removed—but it is the pathway to blessing and authority. This is the rhythm that runs through Scripture: grace that cleanses first, then obedience that flows from gratitude and security in that cleansing. Joshua and his companions are described as "a sign," a living testimony to what God is doing in the world.

That sign points forward to something greater. The vision culminates not with Joshua but with the mysterious figure called "My servant, the Branch," whose arrival will accomplish what Joshua's office merely foreshadows. There is a stone set before Joshua marked with seven eyes—a stone representing completeness and divine surveillance—and on that stone will be engraved an inscription that will remove the iniquity of the land in a single day. This is the work of Christ, the one who will accomplish in reality what the high priesthood could only represent. Through His sacrifice, guilt will be lifted not from one man or one tribe, but from the entire land. When that day comes, the promise continues, there will be peace—neighbors inviting one another under their vines and fig trees, the ancient symbol of security and rest.

What Zechariah shows us is that our acceptance before God is not contingent on our moral excellence or proper performance of religious duty. We stand clothed not in our righteousness but in robes given to us. Yet we also stand positioned to move forward in obedience, secure in that gift, watching and waiting for the Branch who will accomplish the final removal of iniquity. The reflection in your own life is urgent: Where are you still trying to clean your own garments? And having been cleansed by grace, are you walking in His ways not to earn your place, but because you belong to Him?
